html:
Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie, born October 15, 1622, in Reval (now Tallinn), Estonia, and died on April 26, 1686, at Venngarns Castle near Sigtuna, Stockholm County (Uppsala), was a Swedish count, Lord Marshal of the Realm, High Constable of Sweden, Chancellor of the Realm, and poet. He was the son of Jakob De la Gardie and Ebba Brahe. He is buried in Varnhem Monastery Church in Varnhem, Västergötland.

== Biography == Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie studied at Uppsala University and Leiden University, and spent some time in France. Upon his return to Sweden, he participated in Torstensson's war and became a colonel in the Royal Life Guards in 1645. He was considered handsome, charming, educated, and witty, and won the favor of Queen Christina, who bestowed upon him high honors and significant estates. According to some rumors, which are likely false, he may have been the queen's lover. However, historian Peter Ullgren notes that De la Gardie suffered from the loss of the queen's attention during his time in Paris. In 1646, he traveled to France as an extraordinary ambassador, where, in addition to his diplomatic duties, he attended balls, soirees, parties, and receptions. During his time in Paris, he made contacts with the elite of French society and foreign dignitaries on similar missions. De la Gardie was careless with his money during the ambassadorship, which resulted in significant debt. He was responsible for 263,683 silver coins (approximately 7.4 million Swedish krona in 2015 value) for the ambassadorship, but he also spent 100,000 riksdaler that were intended to support the Swedish armies. In addition, he had depleted his own personal funds. In 1647, he became a member of the Royal Council with a seat in the War College. In 1653, De la Gardie lost the queen's favor following a widespread smear campaign and was suspended from his offices and banished from court in December of the same year. With Charles X Gustav's ascension to the throne, De la Gardie regained royal favor, partly because Charles X Gustav was the brother of De la Gardie's wife, Maria Eufrosyne of Palatinate.

His marriage to Maria Eufrosyne of Palatinate took place in 1647, and the wedding celebration was held at Stockholm Castle on March 7, 1647, organized by Queen Christina. Maria received a dowry of 20,000 guilders, and Magnus gave her a morning gift consisting of shares in the barony of Ekholmen and the county of Läckö. The queen also granted Maria the royal estate of Höjentorp, which included 54 farms. Together, they had children: Gustaf Adolf De la Gardie (1647–1695), Catharina Charlotta De la Gardie (Magnusdotter) (1655–1697), and Hedvig Ebba De la Gardie (Magnusdotter) (1657–1700).

In 1648, De la Gardie became a general in Germany and participated in the siege of Prague, where on October 3rd, he led several brigades in an attack against the city gates. The Swedes managed to enter through the gate but were repelled after a couple of hours of fierce close combat. On Saturday, October 14th, 1648, a peace treaty was signed in Osnabrück, ending the hostilities.

In the aftermath of the peace, Swedish soldiers were rewarded with monetary sums. Magnus Gabriel personally received 45,000 riksdaler (approximately 17 million kronor according to 2015 values). Karl X Gustav (then Generalissimo of the army) received 60,000 riksdaler, and Carl Gustaf Wrangel received 80,000.

In 1649, De la Gardie became Governor-General of Livonia, in 1651 he was appointed Field Marshal and High Constable, and in 1652 he became Lord High Treasurer. He also served as Governor-General of Västergötland from 1654 and as Chancellor of Uppsala University. In January 1655, he was reappointed Lord High Treasurer, but due to being deemed unsuitable and more interested in a military career, he was instead appointed again as Governor-General of Livonia in June of the same year, with the task of serving as the king's lieutenant and overseeing the entire war effort in the Baltic provinces. He was also tasked with leading the Livonian army into Poland to join forces with Swedish troops coming from Pomerania. In carrying out this mission, he demonstrated more diplomatic than military skill. The Lithuanians, dissatisfied with Poland and threatened by a Russian attack, were persuaded to voluntarily join Sweden, but due to De la Gardie's failure to expedite negotiations through armed pressure, the agreement was delayed so long that the Russians managed to occupy Vilnius and Kovno, thereby threatening the Swedish advance route. The army, which had already left Riga in mid-June, could only unite with the king's army in Elbing in December. He more clearly demonstrated his lack of military ability during the defense of the Baltic provinces against the Russians in 1656–1657. Due to reckless decisions, Livonia was exposed, and only due to the enemy's inactivity was a conquest avoided. [Source needed] This caused considerable displeasure with the king, and he was relieved of his command in December 1657. From 1659 to 1660, he served as the first envoy at the Oliva Congress.

Upon Karl X Gustav's death, Magnus Gabriel was appointed Lord High Chancellor in accordance with an appointment made the day before the king's death in 1660, but he was only officially recognized as such after the estates confirmed his election in October 1660. According to the king's will, he became a member of Karl XI's regency council.

How certain can we be about this?

Figures from the era of great powers are extensively documented in archives and contemporary publications. Much of what is known about who they were and what they did is well-established; the uncertainty lies in interpretation and motivation.
tldr: Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie was a powerful Swedish nobleman, military figure, and poet during the 17th century. He held high offices and was close to Queen Christina, but he was sometimes criticized for being more interested in festivities and diplomacy than in warfare.
lattlast:
Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie was born in 1622 and became a very influential figure in Sweden. He was both a high-ranking military leader and an educated poet. He held many important titles, such as Marshal of the Realm and Lord Chancellor, which meant he had significant responsibility for the governance and defense of the state.

He was very close to Queen Christina and, as a result, received generous gifts and prestigious positions. However, during his time in France as an ambassador, he spent a great deal of money on parties and social events, leading him into debt. Despite this, he later regained the trust of the kings when they ascended to the throne.

As a military leader, he commanded Swedish forces in several wars, including in Germany and against the Russians. However, he was not always successful on the battlefield, as he sometimes made poor decisions or acted too slowly. Nevertheless, he was a skilled diplomat who could negotiate with other countries.

De la Gardie married Maria Eufrosyne of Palatinate and had three children with her. He died in 1686 at his castle in Uppland and is now buried in Varnhem. His life is well-documented in historical archives, although it can sometimes be difficult to know exactly what he thought or felt at certain times.
